items.definition: The field studying spaces via open sets, continuity, convergence, compactness, connectedness, separation, and related invariants.

items.label: Hausdorff space

items.statement: A topology on a set X is a collection of subsets of X containing ∅ and X and closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
items.formal_statement: $$\tau\subseteq \mathcal P(X),\ \varnothing,X\in\tau,\ \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\tau\Rightarrow \bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\tau,\ \text{and } U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\tau\Rightarrow \bigcap_{k=1}^{n}U_k\in\tau.$$
items.definition: Given a set X, a topology τ on X is a specified collection of subsets of X, called open sets, satisfying the topology axioms: it contains ∅ and X, is closed under arbitrary unions, and is closed under finite intersections.
items.intuition: A topology records which subsets are open, thereby encoding local structure and continuity without choosing numerical distances.
equivalent_definitions: closed-set formulation via complements
items.label: topology

items.statement: In a topological space $(X, \mathcal{T})$, a subset $U$ of $X$ is open exactly when $U$ belongs to $\mathcal{T}$.
items.formal_statement: $$U\subseteq X \text{ is open in }(X,\mathcal{T})\Longleftrightarrow U\in\mathcal{T}.$$
items.definition: An open set of a topological space (X, τ) is a subset U⊆X that is an element of the topology τ.
items.intuition: Open sets are the primitive observable neighborhoods of the space; all topological notions are expressed in terms of them.
items.label: open set

items.statement: A subset C of a topological space X is closed exactly when its complement in X is open.
items.formal_statement: $$C\subseteq X \text{ is closed in }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow X\setminus C\in\tau.$$
items.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a subset C⊆X is closed in X if its complement X\C is open in X.
items.intuition: Closed sets are the subsets whose outside is topologically open; they contain their limit behavior as far as the topology detects it.
equivalent_definitions: member of the closed-set system dual to the topology
items.label: closed set

items.statement: A neighborhood of a point x is any subset containing an open set that contains x.
items.formal_statement: $$N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\Longleftrightarrow \exists U\in\tau\ \text{such that}\ x\in U\subseteq N.$$
items.definition: In a topological space (X, τ), a subset N⊆X is a neighborhood of x∈X if there is an open set U∈τ with x∈U⊆N. If N itself is open, it is an open neighborhood.
items.intuition: Neighborhoods are the sets large enough to contain some genuinely open region around the point.
equivalent_definitions: open neighborhood when the neighborhood itself is required to be open
items.label: neighborhood

items.statement: A basis for a topology is a collection of open sets such that every open set is a union of basis elements.
items.formal_statement: $$\mathcal B\subseteq\tau \text{ is a basis for }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow \forall U\in\tau,\ U=\bigcup\{B\in\mathcal B: B\subseteq U\}.$$
items.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a basis is a collection B of open subsets of X such that for every open set U and every point x∈U, there is B∈B with x∈B⊆U. Equivalently, every open set is a union of basis elements.
items.intuition: A basis gives small reusable open pieces from which all open sets are assembled by unions.
equivalent_definitions: covers X and locally refines finite intersections
items.label: basis for a topology

items.statement: A subbasis is a collection of subsets whose finite intersections form a basis for the topology it generates.
items.formal_statement: $$\langle\mathcal S\rangle=\left\{\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\bigcap_{k=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,k}: S_{\lambda,k}\in\mathcal S,\ n_\lambda<\infty\right\};\ \mathcal S \text{ is a subbasis for }\langle\mathcal S\rangle.$$
items.definition: For a set X, a subbasis S is a collection of subsets of X, usually with ⋃S=X, such that the finite intersections of members of S form a basis for the topology generated by S.
items.intuition: A subbasis specifies even simpler open constraints; finite intersections make basic opens, and arbitrary unions make all opens.
equivalent_definitions: coarsest topology containing the subbasis
items.label: subbasis

items.statement: The closure of A in X is the smallest closed subset of X containing A.
items.formal_statement: $$\operatorname{cl}_X(A)=\overline A=\bigcap\{F\subseteq X: A\subseteq F\ \text{and}\ F \text{ is closed in }X\}.$$
items.definition: For A⊆X, the closure cl_X(A) is the intersection of all closed subsets of X that contain A; equivalently, it is the least closed subset of X containing A.
items.intuition: The closure adds all points that cannot be separated from A by open neighborhoods.
equivalent_definitions: $$x\in\overline A$$ iff every neighborhood of $$x$$ intersects $$A$$
items.label: closure

items.statement: The interior of A in X is the largest open subset of X contained in A.
items.formal_statement: $$\operatorname{int}_X(A)=A^\circ=\bigcup\{U\in\tau: U\subseteq A\}.$$
items.definition: For A⊆X, the interior int_X(A) is the union of all open subsets of X contained in A; equivalently, it is the largest open subset of X contained in A.
items.intuition: The interior consists of points around which A contains an entire open neighborhood.
equivalent_definitions: $$x\in\operatorname{int}_X(A)$$ iff $$A$$ is a neighborhood of $$x$$
items.label: interior

items.statement: The boundary of A is the part of the closure of A outside the interior of A.
items.formal_statement: $$\partial_X A=\overline A\setminus A^\circ=\overline A\cap\overline{X\setminus A}.$$
items.definition: For A⊆X, the boundary ∂_X A is cl_X(A)\int_X(A). Equivalently, it is the set of points whose every neighborhood meets both A and X\A.
items.intuition: Boundary points are exactly the points where the topology cannot see a whole neighborhood lying entirely inside A or entirely outside A.
equivalent_definitions: $$\partial_X A=\overline A\cap\overline{X\setminus A}$$
every neighborhood meets both $$A$$ and $$X\setminus A$$
items.label: boundary

items.statement: A subset A of X is dense in X exactly when its closure is all of X.
items.formal_statement: $$A\subseteq X \text{ is dense in }X\Longleftrightarrow \overline A=X\Longleftrightarrow \forall\,\varnothing\ne U\in\tau,\ U\cap A\ne\varnothing.$$
items.definition: For a topological space X, a subset A⊆X is dense in X if cl_X(A)=X. Equivalently, every nonempty open subset of X intersects A.
items.intuition: A dense subset is topologically unavoidable: every nonempty open region of the space contains a point of it.
equivalent_definitions: every nonempty open set intersects the subset
items.label: dense subset

items.statement: A map between topological spaces is continuous exactly when inverse images of open sets are open.
items.formal_statement: $$f:(X,\tau_X)\to(Y,\tau_Y)\text{ is continuous}\Longleftrightarrow \forall V\in\tau_Y,\ f^{-1}(V)\in\tau_X.$$
items.definition: Given topological spaces (X, τ_X) and (Y, τ_Y), a function f:X→Y is continuous if the preimage of every open subset of Y is open in X.
items.intuition: Continuity means that topological tests made by open sets in the codomain pull back to valid open tests in the domain.
equivalent_definitions: preimages of closed sets are closed
neighborhood formulation at each point
items.label: continuity

items.statement: A homeomorphism is a bijective continuous map whose inverse is also continuous.
items.formal_statement: $$f:X\to Y\text{ is a homeomorphism}\Longleftrightarrow f\text{ is bijective},\ f\text{ is continuous},\ \text{and }f^{-1}:Y\to X\text{ is continuous}.$$
items.definition: For topological spaces X and Y, a homeomorphism is a bijection f:X→Y such that both f and its inverse f^{-1}:Y→X are continuous. If such a map exists, X and Y are homeomorphic.
items.intuition: A homeomorphism is an isomorphism of topological spaces: it preserves and reflects exactly the open-set structure.
equivalent_definitions: bijective open continuous map
bijective closed continuous map
items.label: homeomorphism

items.statement: A net converges to x when it is eventually contained in every neighborhood of x.
items.formal_statement: $$(x_i)_{i\in I}\to x\Longleftrightarrow \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists i_0\in I\ \forall i\ge i_0,\ x_i\in N.$$
items.definition: In a topological space X, a net (x_i) indexed by a directed set I converges to x∈X if for every neighborhood N of x, the net is eventually in N. For sequences, I=ℕ with its usual order.
items.intuition: Convergence means that every topological test neighborhood of the proposed limit eventually contains all later terms.
equivalent_definitions: sequence version when the directed set is $$\mathbb N$$
items.label: convergence

items.statement: A topological space is compact when every open cover has a finite subcover.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal U\subseteq\tau,\ \left(X\subseteq\bigcup_{U\in\mathcal U}U\Rightarrow \exists U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\mathcal U\text{ with }X=\bigcup_{k=1}^{n}U_k\right).$$
items.definition: A topological space X is compact if every family of open subsets whose union contains X has a finite subfamily whose union still contains X. A subset A⊆X is compact when it is compact with the subspace topology.
items.intuition: Compactness says that any open-set description of the whole space already has a finite part that describes the whole space.
equivalent_definitions: compactness_fip_characterization
items.label: compactness

items.statement: A space is compact exactly when every closed family with the finite intersection property has nonempty total intersection.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal F\subseteq\{F\subseteq X:F\text{ closed}\},\ \left[\left(\forall F_1,\ldots,F_n\in\mathcal F,\ \bigcap_{k=1}^{n}F_k\ne\varnothing\right)\Rightarrow \bigcap_{F\in\mathcal F}F\ne\varnothing\right].$$
items.definition: The finite intersection property characterization says that compactness is equivalent to the following closed-set condition: whenever a family of closed subsets has nonempty intersection for every finite subfamily, the whole family has nonempty intersection.
items.intuition: This is the closed-set dual of the open-cover definition: failure of a finite subcover corresponds to finite intersections remaining nonempty.
items.notation: $$\mathcal F$$ has the finite intersection property (FIP)
equivalent_definitions: open-cover definition of compactness
items.label: finite intersection property characterization

items.statement: A topological space is sequentially compact when every sequence has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in the space.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ sequentially compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall (x_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\in X^{\mathbb N},\ \exists n_1<n_2<\cdots,\ \exists x\in X\text{ such that }x_{n_k}\to x.$$
items.definition: A topological space X is sequentially compact if every sequence in X has a subsequence that converges to a point of X.
items.intuition: Sequential compactness is a sequence-based finiteness property; in general topology it is not equivalent to open-cover compactness without additional hypotheses.
items.label: sequential compactness

items.statement: A topological space is connected when it cannot be separated into two disjoint nonempty open subsets.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ connected}\Longleftrightarrow \nexists U,V\in\tau\ \text{with }U,V\ne\varnothing,\ U\cap V=\varnothing,\ \text{and }X=U\cup V.$$
items.definition: A topological space X is connected if there do not exist disjoint nonempty open subsets U,V⊆X whose union is X. Equivalently, the only subsets of X that are both open and closed are ∅ and X.
items.intuition: Connectedness means the topology does not split the whole space into two separated open pieces.
equivalent_definitions: only clopen subsets are $$\varnothing$$ and $$X$$
items.label: connectedness

items.statement: A topological space is path connected when every two points can be joined by a continuous path.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ path connected}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ \exists \gamma:[0,1]\to X\text{ continuous with }\gamma(0)=x,\ \gamma(1)=y.$$
items.definition: A topological space X is path connected if for every pair of points x,y∈X there is a continuous map γ:[0,1]→X from the unit interval to X with γ(0)=x and γ(1)=y.
items.intuition: Path connectedness is connectedness witnessed by actual continuous paths between points; it is stronger than connectedness in general.
equivalent_definitions: there is a single path component
items.label: path connectedness

items.statement: Separation axioms are topological conditions that distinguish points and closed sets by open sets or neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement:
$$T_0: orall x
e y\,\exists U\in	au\,[(x\in U\land y
otin U)\lor(y\in U\land x
otin U)],\quad T_2: orall x
e y\,\exists U,V\in	au\,(x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
items.definition: Separation axioms are a family of properties, including T0, T1, Hausdorff/T2, regularity, and normality, that measure how well a topology separates points from points or points/closed sets from closed sets using open sets.
items.intuition: They quantify how much the topology can distinguish different points or closed regions.
items.label: separation axioms

items.statement: A topological space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: $$(X,\tau)\text{ is Hausdorff}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ x\ne y\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
items.definition: A topological space X is Hausdorff, or T2, if for every pair of distinct points x,y∈X there are disjoint open sets U and V with x∈U and y∈V.
items.intuition: Hausdorffness says distinct points can be topologically pulled apart by nonoverlapping open regions.
equivalent_definitions: the diagonal $$\Delta_X\subseteq X\times X$$ is closed, when product topology is available
items.label: Hausdorff property

items.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is regular if points and disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: $$(X,\tau)\text{ regular}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall x\in X\ \forall F\subseteq X,\ (F\text{ closed}\land x\notin F)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ F\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
items.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is regular if singletons are closed and, whenever F is closed and x∉F, there are disjoint open sets U,V with x∈U and F⊆V.
items.intuition: Regularity separates a point from a closed obstacle by disjoint open regions.
equivalent_definitions: for every point x and neighborhood N of x, there is an open U with x∈U⊆cl(U)⊆N
items.label: regularity

items.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is normal if disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: $$(X,\tau)\text{ normal}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall F,G\subseteq X,\ (F,G\text{ closed}\land F\cap G=\varnothing)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (F\subseteq U,\ G\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
items.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is normal if singletons are closed and every pair of disjoint closed subsets F,G⊆X has disjoint open neighborhoods U,V with F⊆U and G⊆V.
items.intuition: Normality is a strong separation property for closed sets, not just for individual points.
equivalent_definitions: Urysohn-function separation for normal spaces, in the setting of Urysohn's lemma
items.label: normality

items.statement: Countability axioms require bases or local neighborhood bases to be countable.
items.formal_statement: $$\text{First countable: }\forall x\in X\,\exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ (|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \text{and }\mathcal B_x\text{ is local at }x);\quad \text{Second countable: }\exists\mathcal B\text{ basis for }\tau,\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0.$$
items.definition: Countability axioms are topological size restrictions on local or global bases, most commonly first countability and second countability.
items.intuition: They say that the topology can be probed using only countably many local or global open pieces.
items.label: countability axioms

items.statement: A space is first countable if each point has a countable neighborhood basis.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ first countable}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ \left(|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \land\ \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists B\in\mathcal B_x,\ x\in B\subseteq N\right).$$
items.definition: A topological space X is first countable if for every point x∈X there is a countable collection of neighborhoods of x such that every neighborhood of x contains one of them.
items.intuition: First countability gives each point a countable local scale of neighborhoods, making sequences more informative.
equivalent_definitions: each point has a countable local base
items.label: first countability

items.statement: A space is second countable if its topology has a countable basis.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ second countable}\Longleftrightarrow \exists\mathcal B\subseteq\tau\ \left(\mathcal B\text{ is a basis for }\tau\ \land\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0\right).$$
items.definition: A topological space (X, τ) is second countable if there exists a countable basis for τ.
items.intuition: Second countability gives a countable global vocabulary for all open sets.
items.label: second countability

items.statement: A space is locally compact if every point has a compact neighborhood.
items.formal_statement: $$X\text{ locally compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \text{such that }N\text{ is compact as a subspace of }X.$$
items.definition: A topological space X is locally compact if each point x∈X has a neighborhood N that is compact in the subspace topology. In Hausdorff settings this is often equivalently expressed using neighborhoods with compact closure.
items.intuition: Local compactness means that around every point there is a topologically finite region, even if the whole space is not compact.
equivalent_definitions: in Hausdorff spaces, each point has a neighborhood with compact closure
items.label: local compactness

items.statement:
Let $f: \mathbb{D}^n \to \mathbb{D}^n$ be a continuous map from the (unit) disk $\mathbb{D}^n$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to itself. Then $f$ has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point $x \in \mathbb{D}^n$ such that $f(x) = x$.

For $n = 1$ this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map $f: [0, 1] \to [0, 1]$ must cross the diagonal $y = x$ for some $x^* \in [0, 1]$. Hence, $f(x^*) = x^*$.
items.formal_statement: $$f: \mathbb{D}^n \to \mathbb{D}^n \text{ continuous} \implies \exists x \in \mathbb{D}^n, f(x) = x.$$
items.definition: A fixed point of a function $f: X \to X$ is a point $x \in X$ such that $f(x) = x$. The Brouwer fixed point theorem states that any continuous function from the n-dimensional disk to itself has at least one fixed point.
items.intuition: Intuitively, if you have a continuous function that maps a disk to itself, you can imagine it as a deformation of the disk. No matter how you stretch or twist the disk, there will always be at least one point that ends up in the same place after the deformation.
items.label: Brouwer fixed point theorem

hypotheses: C is a simple closed curve in the plane
items.conclusion: R^2\C has exactly two connected components with common boundary C.
items.label: Jordan curve theorem

items.strategy: Use product subbases and Alexander subbase theorem; choice appears through maximal or coordinatewise selection.
items.label: Proof of Tychonoff via Alexander subbase theorem

items.claim: It suffices to test compactness on subbasic covers.
items.justification: Apply Alexander subbase theorem to the product subbasis.
items.label: Tychonoff step: reduce to subbasic cover

items.claim: Coherent choices produce a point avoiding a cover when no finite subcover exists.
items.justification: Use choice or an equivalent maximality argument.
items.label: Tychonoff step: choose coordinates

items.claim: Compactness of factors contradicts failure of finite subcover.
items.justification: Project subbasic data to factors and extract finite subcovers.
items.label: Tychonoff step: finite subcover contradiction

items.strategy: Reduce closed bounded subsets of R^n to compact cubes and finite subcovers.
items.label: Proof of Heine-Borel by open covers

items.claim: A bounded Euclidean set lies in a compact cube.
items.justification: Use interval compactness and finite subcovers.
items.label: Heine-Borel step: compact cube

items.claim: Closed subsets of compact Euclidean spaces are compact.
items.justification: Convert covers by using the open complement of a closed set.
items.label: Heine-Borel step: closed subset

3. Topological spaces
3.1 Definition and examples
Topological spaces are spaces constructed to support continuous maps. The definition is as follows.
Definition 3.1 (Topological spaces) A topological space is a set 𝑋 together with a collection
 of subsets of 𝑋 that are called open in 𝑋, such that the following properties hold.
T1 The subsets ∅ and 𝑋 are in  .
T2 The union of the elements of any subcollection of  is in  .
T3 The intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of  is in  .
A topological space is strictly speaking an ordered pair (𝑋,  ). We refer to  as the topology on 𝑋. We
will often omit specific mention of  if no confusion will arise.
The following theorem states that every metric space (𝑋, 𝑑) is a topological space with the metric
topology 𝑑 on 𝑋.
Theorem 3.2 (Metric spaces are topological spaces) Let (𝑋, 𝑑) be a metric space. Let 𝑑
be the collection of subsets 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 with the property that 𝑈 ∈ 𝑑 if and only if for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈
there is an 𝑟 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈 . Then 𝑑 defines a topology on 𝑋.
Proof. Clearly, ∅ ∈ 𝑑 . To show that 𝑋 ∈ 𝑑 , note that for any 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝖡(𝑥; 1) ⊆ 𝑋. Hence, 𝑋 ∈ 𝑑 .
Thus T1 is satisfied.
⋃
Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be any subcollection of 𝑑 . We need to prove that 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑑 . Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑉 .
⋃
From 𝑉 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 there is 𝜆 ∈ Λ such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝜆 . By the property of 𝑈𝜆 satisfied by the 𝑈𝜆 in 𝑑
there is an 𝑟 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈𝜆 . Hence, 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑉 . Thus 𝑉 ∈ 𝑑 , and T2 is satisfied.
We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of 𝑑 is also an element of
𝑑 . Let 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 . We need to prove that 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 . Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 .
Since 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 , we have that 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. By the defining property
of 𝑑 there is an 𝑟𝑖 > 0 such that 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟𝑖 ) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Let 𝑟 = min{𝑟1 , 𝑟2 , … , 𝑟𝑛 }. Then
𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟𝑖 ) ⊆ 𝑈𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2, … , 𝑛. Thus 𝖡(𝑥; 𝑟) ⊆ 𝑈1 ∩𝑈2 ∩⋯∩𝑈𝑛 , and so 𝑈1 ∩𝑈2 ∩⋯∩𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑑 .
Hence, T3 is satisfied.
The following theorem relates the metric topologies for two equivalent metrics.
Theorem 3.3 Let 𝑋 be any set, and let 𝑑1 and 𝑑2 be two equivalent metrics on 𝑋. Then
𝑑1 = 𝑑2 .

14 3.1. Definition and examples
This follows from Exercise 2.7.
Example 3.4 (Discrete topology) Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  of all subsets of 𝑋, i.e.
the power set (𝑋) of 𝑋, is a topology on 𝑋. We refer to this topology as the discrete topology.
A set 𝑋 equipped with the discrete topology is referred to as a discrete topological space.
The discrete topology is the unique topology where the singletons are open. We can think of a
discrete topological space as a space of separate, isolated points, with no close interaction between
different points.
For any set 𝑋, the discrete topology is the largest topology we may equip 𝑋 with. The smallest
topology is called the indiscrete topology.
Example 3.5 (Indiscrete topology) Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  consisting of ∅ and
𝑋 is a topology on 𝑋, referred to as the indiscrete topology on 𝑋. A set 𝑋 equipped with the
indiscrete topology is referred to as an indiscrete topological space.
Example 3.6 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. The following collections all define a topology on 𝑋.
(1) 1 = ind = {∅, 𝑋}
(2) 2 = {∅, {𝑎}, 𝑋}
(3) 3 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋}
(4) 4 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋}
(5) 5 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, 𝑋}
(6) 6 = disc = (𝑋) = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑐}, {𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, 𝑋}
There are in total 29 topologies on 𝑋. However, there are also collections of subsets of 𝑋
which do not define topologies on 𝑋. None of the following collections of subsets of 𝑋 define
a topology on 𝑋.
(1) {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑏}, 𝑋}
(2) {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑐}, 𝑋}
(3) {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑏, 𝑐}, 𝑋}
Definition 3.7 (Comparable topologies) Let 𝑋 be any set and suppose that 1 and 2 are two
topologies on 𝑋. If 1 ⊆ 2 , we say that 1 is coarser than 2 and that 2 is finer than 1 . We
say that 1 and 2 are comparable if either 1 ⊆ 2 or 2 ⊆ 1 .
Clearly, for any set 𝑋, the discrete topology disc contains the indiscrete topology ind : disc ⊇ ind .
Hence, the discrete topology is finer than the indiscrete topology and the indiscrete topology is coarser
than the discrete topology.
Example 3.8 (Cofinite topology) Let 𝑋 be any set. The collection  of subsets of 𝑋 consisting
of subsets 𝑈 ⊆ 𝑋 such that 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 is either finite or all of 𝑋 is a topology on 𝑋. We refer
to this topology as the cofinite topology on 𝑋.
Chapter 3. Topological spaces 15
If 𝑋 is a finite set, the cofinite topology is equal to the discrete topology. If 𝑋 is an infinite set, the
cofinite topology is strictly coarser than the discrete topology, in the sense that the cofinite topology
is properly contained in the discrete topology.
We end this section with a theorem that we can use to prove that some set is open. To state the
theorem we need the following definition.
Definition 3.9 (Neighborhoods) Let 𝑋 be a topological space, 𝑈 a subset of 𝑋 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋.
We say that 𝑈 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 if 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑈 is open in 𝑋.
A neighborhood in the sense of the previous definition is sometimes referred to as an open neigh-
borhood, cf. Definition 2.11.
Theorem 3.10 Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 is open in 𝑋 if and only if for
every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a neighborhood 𝑈𝑥 of 𝑥 such that 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 .
Proof. Assume that 𝑈 is open in 𝑋. Then for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 , 𝑈 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 that is contained
in 𝑈 .
We prove the other implication. Assume that for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 there is a 𝑈𝑥 ∈  such that
𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 , i.e., that 𝑈𝑥 is a neighborhood of 𝑥 such that 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . To prove that 𝑈 ∈  , we will
⋃ ⋃
prove that 𝑈 = 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . Assume that 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑈𝑥′ . Then 𝑥′ ∈ 𝑈𝑥′ ⊆ 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . Furthermore, any
⋃
point in 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 is in 𝑈𝑥 for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 so by assumption, 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈𝑥 ⊆ 𝑈 . Hence,
⋃
𝑈 = 𝑥∈𝑈 𝑈𝑥 . As 𝑈 is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}. Show that  = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}, 𝑋} is

 ⊆  ′.

items.label: Exercise 3.2
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $X$ be non-empty, fix $x_0\in X$, and define $\mathcal{T}=\{U\subseteq X\mid x_0\notin U\text{ or }X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}$.

### Solution

$\emptyset\in\mathcal{T}$ because it omits $x_0$, and $X\in\mathcal{T}$ because $X\setminus X=\emptyset$ is finite. For arbitrary unions, let $V=\bigcup U_\lambda$. If $x_0\notin V$, then $V\in\mathcal{T}$. If $x_0\in V$, then $x_0\in U_{\lambda_0}$ for some $\lambda_0$, so $X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite and $X\setminus V\subseteq X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite. For finite intersections $W=\bigcap_{i=1}^nU_i$, if some $U_i$ omits $x_0$, so does $W$; otherwise every $X\setminus U_i$ is finite and $X\setminus W=\bigcup_i(X\setminus U_i)$ is finite. Thus $\mathcal{T}$ is a topology.

items.label: Exercise 3.3
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Define the coarsest topology on 𝑋
such that 𝐴 is open in 𝑋.
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $X$ be a set and $A\subseteq X$. Coarser means contained as a collection of open sets.

### Solution

The coarsest topology on $X$ in which $A$ is open is $\mathcal{T}_A=\{\emptyset,A,X\}$. This is a topology because all unions and finite intersections of these three sets are again among these three sets. If $\mathcal{T}$ is any topology with $A\in\mathcal{T}$, then $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$ by the topology axioms, so $\mathcal{T}_A\subseteq\mathcal{T}$. Hence $\mathcal{T}_A$ is the desired coarsest topology.

items.label: Exercise 3.4
items.statement:
Show that the discrete topology disc is finer than the cofinite topology cof on
any set 𝑋.
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

$\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$ and $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}=\{U\subseteq X\mid X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}\cup\{\emptyset\}$.

### Solution

Every $U\in\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}$ is a subset of $X$, and every subset of $X$ lies in $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Therefore $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$, so the discrete topology is finer. If $X$ is infinite this containment is strict because singletons are discrete-open but not cofinite-open; if $X$ is finite the two topologies agree.

items.label: Exercise 3.5
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑}. Find two topologies 1 and 2 with 1 ≠ 2 such that a
bijection 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑋 is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given 1 as topology and the
codomain is given 2 as topology).
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $X=\{a,b,c,d\}$. A homeomorphism is a bijection whose map and inverse are continuous.

### Solution

Let $f$ swap $a$ and $b$ and fix $c,d$. Set $\mathcal{T}_1=\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{a,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$ and $\mathcal{T}_2=\{f(U)\mid U\in\mathcal{T}_1\}=\{\emptyset,\{b\},\{b,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$. Both are topologies by direct closure under unions and finite intersections. They are distinct since $\{a\}\in\mathcal{T}_1$ but $\{a\}\notin\mathcal{T}_2$. For each open set in $\mathcal{T}_2$, its preimage under $f$ is the corresponding open set in $\mathcal{T}_1$; since $f=f^{-1}$, the inverse is continuous by the same check. Thus the two spaces are homeomorphic with unequal topologies.

items.label: Exercise 3.6
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of 𝑋.
(a) Assume that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵. Show that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵.
(b) Show that 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵.
Chapter 3. Topological spaces 21
We say that a set 𝐴 intersects or meets a set 𝐵 if 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ≠ ∅.
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $X$ be a topological space and $A,B\subseteq X$. The closure $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$.

### Solution

If $A\subseteq B$, every closed set containing $B$ also contains $A$, so $\overline{A}$ is contained in every closed superset of $B$ and hence $\overline{A}\subseteq\overline{B}$. For unions, the first part gives $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}\subseteq\overline{A\cup B}$. Conversely $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$ is closed as a finite union of closed sets and contains $A\cup B$, so minimality gives $\overline{A\cup B}\subseteq\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$. Therefore $\overline{A\cup B}=\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$.

items.label: Exercise 3.7
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Show that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 if
and only if every neighborhood of 𝑥 intersects 𝐴.
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $A\subseteq X$. A neighbourhood of $x$ is an open set containing $x$, and $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$.

### Solution

If some neighbourhood $U$ of $x$ satisfies $U\cap A=\emptyset$, then $A\subseteq X\setminus U$, where $X\setminus U$ is closed. Hence $\overline{A}\subseteq X\setminus U$, so $x\notin\overline{A}$. Conversely, if $x\notin\overline{A}$, then there is a closed set $C\supseteq A$ with $x\notin C$. Then $U=X\setminus C$ is an open neighbourhood of $x$ and $U\cap A=\emptyset$. Thus $x\in\overline{A}$ iff every neighbourhood of $x$ meets $A$.

4. Generating topologies
4.1 Generating topologies from subsets
The following theorem tells us how we may extract a third topology from two other topologies on the
same set.
Theorem 4.1 (The intersection of two topologies is a topology) Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 1
and 2 be two topologies on 𝑋. Then 1 ∩ 2 is also a topology on 𝑋.
Proof. Clearly, ∅ and 𝑋 are in 1 ∩ 2 , so T1 is satisfied.
Let {𝑈𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of sets such that 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ where Λ is some
⋃
index set. Then, for 𝑖 = 1, 2, 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑖 for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Thus 𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2. Hence,
⋃
𝜆∈Λ 𝑈𝜆 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 , and so, T2 is satisfied.
Let {𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 } be a finite subcollection of  . We need to prove that T3 is satisfied, i.e.,
𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈  . Note that if 𝑈1 , 𝑈2 , … , 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑖 then 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 𝑖 for 𝑖 = 1, 2.
Hence, 𝑈1 ∩ 𝑈2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ 𝑈𝑛 ∈ 1 ∩ 2 . Thus T3 is satisfied.

Example 4.2 Let 𝑋 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, and let 1 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, 𝑋} and 2 = {∅, {𝑐}, 𝑋} be two
topologies on 𝑋. Then
1 ∩ 2 = {∅, 𝑋}
is the indiscrete topology on 𝑋 while
1 ∪ 2 = {∅, {𝑎}, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑐}, 𝑋}
is not a topology on 𝑋: 1 ∪ 2 does not satisfy T2.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 23
Recall from Section 3.1 that for any set 𝑋 the discrete topology disc is the largest topology we
may equip 𝑋 with, and the indiscrete topology ind is the smallest topology we may equip 𝑋 with. For
any topology  on 𝑋 we have
ind ⊆  ⊆ disc .
That is, we have partially ordered topologies on 𝑋 by inclusion.
Let 𝑋 be a set. We often want to have a collection of subsets  of 𝑋 to be the open subsets of a
topology on 𝑋.
Definition 4.3 (Topology generated by a collection of subsets) Let 𝑋 be a set, and let 
be a collection of subsets of 𝑋. The topology generated by  is the topology
⋂
⟨⟩ = 
 topology
⊆
on 𝑋.
In other words, ⟨⟩ contains  and for any other topology  ′ containing , we have ⟨⟩ ⊆  ′ .
Thus ⟨⟩ is unique.
Example 4.4 Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  = ∅. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the indiscrete topology
on 𝑋, i.e.,
⟨⟩ = ind = {∅, 𝑋}.
Example 4.5 Let 𝑋 be a set, and let  be the collection of all the singletons of 𝑋, i.e.,
 = {{𝑥} ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋}. Then ⟨⟩ is the same as the discrete topology on 𝑋, i.e.,
⟨⟩ = disc = (𝑋).
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions

Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology and $S=\{a,b\}$. A set is dense iff its closure is all of $X$.

### Solution

Closed sets are complements of listed open sets. The closed sets containing $S$ are exactly $X$ and $\{a,b,c\}$, so $\overline{S}=X\cap\{a,b,c\}=\{a,b,c\}$. This is not $X$, so $S$ is not dense. Equivalently, the non-empty open set $\{d,e\}$ is disjoint from $S$.

Chapter 4. Generating topologies 27
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology $\mathcal{T}$.

### Solution

For $\mathcal{S}=\{\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\}\}$, the union is $X$. Its finite intersections give basis elements $\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\},\{b\},\{d,e\}$, whose unions are exactly the members of $\mathcal{T}$. Thus $\mathcal{S}$ is a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$. For $\mathcal{S}'=\{\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\}\}$, the generated basis is $\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\},\{b\}$. Unions of these sets cannot produce $\{c,d,e\}$, which is in $\mathcal{T}$, so $\mathcal{S}'$ is not a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$.

items.label: Exercise 4.2
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all open intervals (𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑥 < 𝑏} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
standard topology on ℝ denoted by std .
(b) Show that std = 𝑑 where 𝑑 is the metric topology obtained from the metric 𝑑(𝑥, 𝑦) =
|𝑥 − 𝑦|.
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $\mathcal{B}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{R},a<b\}$.

### Solution

For every $x$, $(x-1,x+1)$ contains $x$, so B1 holds. If $x\in(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, then $x\in(\max(a,c),\min(b,d))\subseteq(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, so B2 holds. Thus $\mathcal{B}$ is a basis. Metric balls for $|x-y|$ are intervals $(x-r,x+r)$, and every interval around a point contains such a ball by choosing $r=\min(x-a,b-x)$. Hence this basis generates the usual metric topology.

items.label: Exercise 4.3
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $\mathcal{S}=\{(a,\infty)\mid a\in\mathbb{R}\}\cup\{(-\infty,b)\mid b\in\mathbb{R}\}$.

### Solution

The union of the subbasis elements is $\mathbb{R}$. Finite intersections are rays, empty sets, or intervals $(a,b)=(a,\infty)\cap(-\infty,b)$. Thus every standard basis interval is generated. Conversely, each ray is a union of standard open intervals, e.g. $(a,\infty)=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty(a,a+n)$. Therefore the topology generated by $\mathcal{S}$ is exactly the standard topology.

items.statement:
Let ℚ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let ℝ denote the set of real numbers.
Show that
 = {(𝑎, 𝑏) ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑏, 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℚ}
is a basis for the standard topology on ℝ where (𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑎 < 𝑥 < 𝑏}.
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $\mathcal{B}_{\mathbb{Q}}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{Q},a<b\}$.

### Solution

Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives rational $a<x<b$ for each real $x$, so B1 holds. Intersections of rational-endpoint intervals are rational-endpoint intervals, so B2 holds. Every such interval is standard-open. Conversely, if $x\in(a,b)$ with real endpoints, choose rationals $p,q$ with $a<p<x<q<b$; then $(p,q)\subseteq(a,b)$. Hence these intervals generate the standard topology.

items.label: Exercise 4.5
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [𝑎, 𝑏) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣
𝑎 ⩽ 𝑥 < 𝑏} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
lower limit topology on ℝ.
(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of ℝ given the lower limit topology.
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $\mathcal{B}_\ell=\{[a,b)\mid a<b\}$ and let $\mathcal{T}_\ell$ be the lower limit topology.

### Solution

B1 follows from $x\in[x,x+1)$. B2 follows from $[a,b)\cap[c,d)=[\max(a,c),\min(b,d))$ when the intersection is non-empty. The closure of $(0,1)$ is $[0,1)$: first, $[0,1)$ is closed because its complement $(-\infty,0)\cup[1,\infty)$ is lower-limit-open. Thus the closure is contained in $[0,1)$. Every neighbourhood of $0$ contains some $[0,\varepsilon)$ meeting $(0,1)$, while $[1,2)$ is a neighbourhood of $1$ disjoint from $(0,1)$. Therefore $\overline{(0,1)}=[0,1)$.

Show that the collection  = {𝐵(𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ} is a basis for a topology on ℤ. The topology gen-
erated by  is known as the digital line topology on ℤ. See [1, pp. 44–46] for some applications

items.solution:
### Setup

For $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, let $B(n)=\{n\}$ if $n$ is odd and $B(n)=\{n-1,n,n+1\}$ if $n$ is even.

### Solution

Each $n$ lies in $B(n)$, so B1 holds. For B2, if an intersection contains an odd point $x$, then $B(x)=\{x\}$ refines the intersection. If it contains an even point $x$, then the only basis element with even center $x$ supplies the needed local refinement; neighbouring odd basis elements are singletons and cannot contain $x$. Hence every point in an intersection has a basis neighbourhood contained in that intersection, so the collection is a basis.

items.label: Exercise 4.7
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all subsets of the form 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 = {𝑎𝑧 + 𝑏 ∣ 𝑧 ∈ ℤ} of ℤ,
where 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℤ and 𝑎 ≠ 0. (The set 𝐴𝑎,𝑏 is known as an arithmetic progression.)
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℤ.
(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by . (This
topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on ℤ and it was used originally
by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)
28 4.4. Exercises
items.solution:
### Setup

For $a\ne0$, define $A_{a,b}=\{az+b\mid z\in\mathbb{Z}\}$.

### Solution

B1 holds because $A_{1,0}=\mathbb{Z}$. If $n\in A_{a,b}\cap A_{c,d}$, then the intersection is the congruence class $A_{\operatorname{lcm}(|a|,|c|),n}$, so B2 holds. Each basis element is clopen because its complement is a finite union of the other residue classes modulo $|a|$. Every non-empty open set is infinite. If there were finitely many primes $p_i$, then $\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{-1,1\}=\bigcup_iA_{p_i,0}$ would be closed, so $\{-1,1\}$ would be open, contradicting that no non-empty finite set is open. Therefore there are infinitely many primes.

Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. Show
that a subset 𝐴 of 𝑋 is dense in 𝑋 if and only if every non-empty basis element in  intersects
𝐴. (Recall that a set 𝑈 intersects a set 𝑉 if 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ≠ ∅.)
5. Constructing topological spaces
5.1 Subspaces
Let 𝐴 be a subset of a topological space 𝑋. There is a natural way to define a topology on 𝐴 that is
based on the topology on 𝑋.
Definition 5.1 (Subspace topology) Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of
𝑋. The collection
𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋}
of subsets of 𝐴 is called the subspace topology on 𝐴.
The subspace topology is indeed a topology.
Lemma 5.2 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of 𝑋. Then the collection
𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} is a topology on 𝐴.
Proof. Let  denote the topology on 𝑋.
Since ∅, 𝑋 ∈  , ∅ = 𝐴 ∩ ∅ and 𝐴 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑋, then, clearly, ∅, 𝐴 ∈ 𝐴 . Hence, T1 is satisfied.

Let 𝑋 be a topological space (with topology  ), and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. If 𝑉 is a subset of 𝐴, there
are two possible meanings to the statement “𝑉 is open.” We can either take 𝑉 to be open in 𝑋, i.e., 𝑉 ∈  , or
we can take 𝑉 to be open in 𝐴, i.e., 𝑉 ∈ 𝐴 . In general, these do not mean the same thing.
We say that 𝑉 is closed in 𝐴 if 𝑉 is closed in the subspace topology of 𝐴. In other words, if 𝑉 is closed in
𝐴, then 𝐴 ⧵ 𝑉 is open in 𝐴.

Theorem 5.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subspace of 𝑋. Then a subset 𝐾
of 𝐴 is closed in 𝐴 if and only if there is a closed subset 𝐿 of 𝑋 with 𝐾 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝐿.

open in the subspace 𝐴 but which are not open in 𝑋.
Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 31
Example 5.4 Let ℝ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, cf.
Exercise 4.2, and let 𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ. Then sets of the form [0, 𝑎) and (𝑎, 1] with
0 < 𝑎 < 1 are open in 𝐼 but not in ℝ.
The following theorem describes how we may extract a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴 from
the basis of a topology on 𝑋.
Theorem 5.5 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on 𝑋. If 𝐴
is a subset of 𝑋, then the collection
𝐴 = {𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ∈ }
is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴.
Proof. We need to prove that 𝐴 is a basis for the subspace topology on 𝐴. We first prove that 𝐴 is
a basis for a topology on 𝐴, and then that the topology generated by 𝐴 equals the subspace topology
on 𝐴.
First note that each 𝐵 ∈  is open in 𝑋, and so, each 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ∈ 𝐴 is open in 𝐴. For each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋
there is a basis element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵. Let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑋, there must be a basis
element 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵. Hence, B1 holds. Now let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ) where 𝐵1 and
𝐵2 are basis elements of . Since  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋, it follows that there is a basis
element 𝐵3 of  such that 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵3 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ (𝐵1 ∩ 𝐵2 ). Hence, B2 is satisfied.
Thus 𝐴 is a basis for a topology on 𝐴.
Let 𝐴 be the subspace topology on 𝐴. We want to prove that the topology  ′ generated by 𝐴 is
equal to 𝐴 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 , then, using the fact that  is a basis for the topology on 𝑋,
we have 𝐵 ∈  such that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 . Thus 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∈  ′ , cf. Theorem 4.9. By Theorem 4.10
we know that  ′ is equal to the collection of all unions of elements of 𝐴 . Hence, if 𝑊 ∈  ′ then 𝑊
equals a union of elements of 𝐴 . Since each element of 𝐴 belongs to 𝐴 and 𝐴 is a topology, 𝑊
also belongs to 𝐴 .
We end this section with an alternative description of the subspace topology. Let 𝑋 be a topological
space, and let 𝑇 be a set. There do exist topologies on 𝑇 that make 𝑓 ∶ 𝑇 → 𝑋 continuous, e.g., the
discrete topology. If 𝑓 is the intersection of all topologies on 𝑇 such that 𝑓 is continuous, then 𝑓 is
the coarsest topology for which 𝑓 is continuous and
𝑓 = {𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋}.
From this we may define the subspace topology as follows: Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be
subset of 𝑋. The subspace topology on 𝐴 is then the coarsest topology on 𝐴 for which the inclusion
𝑖 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝑋, given by 𝑖(𝑥) = 𝑥 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴, is continuous. This coincides with our previous definition as
𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 for any subset 𝑈 of 𝑋. Thus
𝑖 = {𝑖−1 (𝑈 ) ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋} = {𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 ∣ 𝑈 is open in 𝑋},
and hence, 𝑖 = 𝐴 .
The following theorem describes a universal property for the subspace topology.

logical space 𝑌 and every map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝐴, 𝑓 is continuous if and only if 𝑖◦𝑓 ∶ 𝑌 → 𝑋 is

Chapter 5. Constructing topological spaces 33
items.solution:
### Setup

Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for $X$ and $A\subseteq X$.

### Solution

If $A$ is dense, every non-empty open set meets $A$, hence every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Conversely, assume every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Given a non-empty open set $U$, choose $x\in U$ and a basis element $B$ with $x\in B\subseteq U$. Then $B\cap A\ne\emptyset$, so $U\cap A\ne\emptyset$. Thus every non-empty open set meets $A$, and $A$ is dense.

items.label: Exercise 5.1

6. Topological properties
6.1 Connected spaces
One of the fundamental results of calculus is the intermediate value theorem. The theorem says that
for a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → ℝ and for a real number 𝑟 between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏) there is a real
number 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟. See Figure 6.1. From this result we can deduce that the graph
of a continuous map (in this setting) is connected.

Definition 6.1 (Connected space) Let 𝑋 be a topological space. A separation of 𝑋 is a pair
of non-empty subsets 𝑈 and 𝑉 that are open in 𝑋, disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋. We say
that 𝑋 is connected if there are no separations of 𝑋. Otherwise it is disconnected.
The property of being connected is a topological property as it is formulated entirely in terms of open subsets.
In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is connected then so is 𝑌 .
Example 6.2 Let 𝑋 be the set {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}. If we equip 𝑋 with the topology 1 =
{∅, {𝑎, 𝑏}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑑, 𝑒}, {𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is disconnected; the pair 𝑈 = {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐} and
𝑉 = {𝑑, 𝑒} is a separation of 𝑋 in this topology.
However, if we equip 𝑋 with the topology 2 = {∅, {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}, {𝑐}, {𝑐, 𝑑, 𝑒}, 𝑋} then it is
connected; there are no separations of 𝑋 in this topology.
45
46 6.1. Connected spaces
Example 6.3 Let 𝑋 be an indiscrete space. Then 𝑋 is connected as there are no separations
of 𝑋, i.e., there are no non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 who are disjoint and whose union equal 𝑋.
Example 6.4 Let 𝑋 be a discrete space containing two or more points. Then 𝑋 is disconnected.
Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝑋 and let 𝑈 = {𝑝} and 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ {𝑝}. Then the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of
𝑋.
Example 6.5 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and let
𝑋 = [0, 1) ∪ (1, 2) be a subspace of ℝ. Since 𝑈 = [0, 1) is open in 𝑋 (but not in ℝ) and
𝑉 = (1, 2) is open 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, they form a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is
disconnected.
In the above examples the spaces that are connected all share the property that the only subsets that
are both open and closed in 𝑋 are ∅ and 𝑋. Likewise, the disconnected spaces all share the property
that there are non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
Theorem 6.6 Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if the are no
non-empty proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there are non-empty
proper subsets of 𝑋 that are both open and closed in 𝑋.
Assume 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋.
Thus 𝑈 is open in 𝑋. Since 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋, we have 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 = 𝑉 . Thus 𝑈 𝑐 is open
in 𝑋, and so, 𝑈 is closed in 𝑋. Hence, 𝑈 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Likewise, 𝑉 is both open and
closed in 𝑋.
Assume that the non-empty proper subset 𝑈 of 𝑋 is both open and closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑉 = 𝑈 𝑐 =
𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 . Then 𝑉 is open in 𝑋, 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 = ∅ and 𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 = 𝑋. Hence, the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of
𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.
Theorem 6.7 Let 𝑋 be a connected space, 𝑌 be a topological space, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be
a surjective continuous map. Then 𝑌 is connected.
Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a surjective continuous map and 𝑌 is
disconnected then 𝑋 is disconnected.
Assume that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let the pair 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation
of 𝑌 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are non-empty subsets of 𝑋 which are open in 𝑋 as 𝑓 is a surjective
continuous map. Furthermore,
𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∩ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ) = ∅ and 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) ∪ 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) = 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ∪ 𝑉 ) = 𝑋.
Hence, the pair 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 ) and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) is a separation of 𝑋. Thus 𝑋 is disconnected.
A subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑍 is connected if 𝐴 is connected in the subspace topology. Thus the
theorem can be extended to saying that the continuous image of a connected space is connected, i.e., assuming
𝑋 is a connected space and 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a continuous map then 𝑓 (𝑋) is connected in 𝑌 .

We may also describe connectedness by way of the following theorem.
Theorem 6.8 Let 𝑋 be a topological space. Then 𝑋 is connected if and only if every contin-
uous map form 𝑋 to a discrete space, with at least two points, is constant.
Proof. We prove the equivalent statement that 𝑋 is disconnected if and only if there is a non-constant
continuous map from 𝑋 to a discrete space (with at least two points).
Assume that 𝑋 is disconnected. Let 𝑈 be a non-empty proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and
closed in 𝑋. Let 𝑌 = {𝑎, 𝑏} be given the discrete topology. The map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 that sends 𝑈 to 𝑎
and 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵ 𝑈 to 𝑏 is continuous and not constant.
Let 𝑌 be a discrete space with at least two points. Assume that 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a non-constant
continuous map. Then for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑦 = 𝑓 (𝑥) for some 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋, 𝑓 −1 ({𝑦}) is a non-empty
proper subset of 𝑋 that is both open and closed in 𝑋. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝑋 is disconnected.
We will prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected. To prove this we
will need the following two results.
Lemma 6.9 Let 𝑋 be a disconnected space with separation 𝑈 and 𝑉 , and let 𝐴 be a connected
subspace of 𝑋. Then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 .
Proof. Since 𝑈 and 𝑉 are open in 𝑋, the intersections 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐴 ∩ 𝑉 are both open in 𝐴 (in the
subspace topology). Furthermore, the complement of 𝐴∩𝑈 in 𝐴 is equal to 𝐴∩𝑉 as 𝑈 𝑐 = 𝑋 ⧵𝑈 = 𝑉 .
Hence, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is also closed in 𝐴. Thus by Theorem 6.6, 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 is either empty or all of 𝐴 as 𝐴 is
connected. If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = ∅ then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑉 . If 𝐴 ∩ 𝑈 = 𝐴 then 𝐴 ⊆ 𝑈 .
Theorem 6.10 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let {𝐴𝜆 }𝜆∈Λ be a collection of connected
⋂ ⋃
subspaces of 𝑋 such that 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is non-empty. Then 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 is connected.
⋃
Proof. Let 𝑌 = 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Suppose that 𝑌 is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of 𝑌 . Let
𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑌 . We will show that this leads to a contradiction, and so, 𝑌 must be
connected.
⋂
Let 𝑝 ∈ 𝜆∈Λ 𝐴𝜆 . Then either 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 or 𝑝 ∈ 𝑉 . Assume without loss of generality that 𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 .
By Lemma 6.9 it follows that for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ either 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 or 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑉 . Since we have assumed that
𝑝 ∈ 𝑈 we must have 𝐴𝜆 ⊆ 𝑈 for all 𝜆 ∈ Λ. Thus 𝑌 ⊆ 𝑈 . But this implies that 𝑉 is empty, and hence,
contradicts that 𝑈 and 𝑉 is a separation of 𝑌 . Thus 𝑌 is connected.
We can prove that the (finite) product of connected spaces is again connected.
Theorem 6.11 Let 𝑋1 , 𝑋2 , … , 𝑋𝑛 be connected spaces. Then the product space 𝑋1 × 𝑋2 ×
⋯ × 𝑋𝑛 is connected.
Proof. We prove the statement for the product of two connected spaces. The general result then follows
by an induction argument.
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two connected spaces. We must prove that 𝑋 × 𝑌 is connected. Since for each
𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the subspace {𝑥} × 𝑌 of 𝑋 × 𝑌 is homeomorphic to 𝑌 , it follows that {𝑥} × 𝑌 is connected.
Similarly, for each 𝑦 ∈ 𝑌 the subspace 𝑋 × {𝑦} is homeomorphic to 𝑋, and hence, 𝑋 × {𝑦} is

({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∪ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is
({𝑥} × 𝑌 ) ∩ (𝑋 × {𝑦}) = {(𝑥, 𝑦)} ≠ ∅.

is connected as it is the union of two connected spaces whose intersection is equal to {(𝑥0 , 𝑦)}. Hence,

The theorem can be extended to hold for arbitrary products, 𝜆∈Λ 𝑋𝜆 , if we equip the product with the

An important example of a connected space is the set of real numbers equipped with the standard
topology.
Theorem 6.12 (The real numbers are connected) Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped
with the standard topology. Then ℝ is connected.
We will use the fact that the real numbers satisfy the following two properties.
(1) Every subset of ℝ that is bounded above has a least upper bound. This is known as the least
upper bound property.
(2) If 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ ℝ with 𝑥 < 𝑦 then there is a real number 𝑧 such that 𝑥 < 𝑧 < 𝑦.
Proof. Assume that ℝ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of ℝ. Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation
of ℝ, and choose 𝑎 ∈ 𝑈 and 𝑏 ∈ 𝑉 . We may assume without loss of generality that 𝑎 < 𝑏.
Let 𝐴 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑈 and 𝐵 = [𝑎, 𝑏] ∩ 𝑉 . Then the pair 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏], with 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴
and 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵. Also note that 𝐴 is bounded above by 𝑏. Hence, by the least upper bound property 𝐴 has
a least upper bound; 𝑐 = sup 𝐴. Thus 𝑎 ⩽ 𝑐 ⩽ 𝑏. We will show that 𝑐 belongs neither to 𝐴 nor to 𝐵
thus contradicting the fact that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏].
Assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐵. Since 𝑎 ∉ 𝐵 and 𝐵 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏], it follows that there is a real number 𝑑
such that 𝑎 < 𝑑 < 𝑐 and (𝑑, 𝑐] ⊆ 𝐵. This implies that 𝑑 is an upper bound of 𝐴 and that 𝑑 is less than
the least upper bound 𝑐. That is a contradiction, and so, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵.
Now assume that 𝑐 ∈ 𝐴. Since 𝐴 is open in [𝑎, 𝑏] and 𝑏 ∉ 𝐴, there is a real number 𝑑 such that
[𝑐, 𝑑) ⊆ 𝐴. For any 𝑒 ∈ (𝑐, 𝑑) it follows that 𝑒 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑒 > 𝑐. That is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐
is an upper bound of 𝐴. Thus 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴.
Hence, 𝑐 ∉ 𝐴 and 𝑐 ∉ 𝐵. This is a contradiction to the fact that 𝑐 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] and that 𝐴 and 𝐵 is a
separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus ℝ must be connected.
As an immediate consequence of Theorem 6.12, we get that open intervals of the form (𝑎, 𝑏),
(−∞, 𝑏) and (𝑎, ∞) are all connected as they are all homeomorphic to ℝ (with the standard topology).
We can also show that every closed interval [𝑎, 𝑏] is connected. Furthermore, by Theorem 6.11 and
Theorem 6.12 ℝ𝑛 is a connected space.
Chapter 6. Topological properties 49
Theorem 6.13 (Generalized intermediate value theorem) Let 𝑋 be a connected space, and
let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ be a continuous map where ℝ is given the standard topology. If 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑋 and
if 𝑟 is a real number that lies between 𝑓 (𝑎) and 𝑓 (𝑏), there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.
Proof. Assume that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋). We will show that this contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected.
By the assumption that 𝑟 ∉ 𝑓 (𝑋), i.e., 𝑓 (𝑋) ⊆ ℝ ⧵ {𝑟} = (−∞, 𝑟) ∪ (𝑟, ∞), we have a separation
of 𝑋:
𝑈 = 𝑓 −1 ((−∞, 𝑟)) and 𝑉 = 𝑓 −1 ((𝑟, ∞))
are disjoint non-empty open subsets of 𝑋 whose union equals 𝑋. Thus they are a separation of 𝑋. This
contradicts the assumption that 𝑋 is connected. Hence, 𝑟 ∈ 𝑓 (𝑋). In other words, there is a 𝑐 ∈ 𝑋
such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟.
We end this section with a discussion of path-connectivity.
Definition 6.14 (Path-connected space) Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥, 𝑦 ∈ 𝑋. A
path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 is a continuous map 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦 where
[𝑎, 𝑏] is a subspace of ℝ with the standard topology. We say that 𝑋 is path-connected if every
pair of points of 𝑋 can be joined by a path in 𝑋.
The property of being path-connected is a topological property as it is completely described using the el-
ements of 𝑋 and its open subsets. In other words, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and 𝑋 is
path connected then so is 𝑌 .
Example 6.15 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology. Then ℝ
is path-connected as for any two points, 𝑝, 𝑞 ∈ ℝ, there is a path from 𝑝 to 𝑞 in ℝ, e.g., the path
given by 𝑓 (𝑡) = (1 − 𝑡)𝑝 + 𝑡𝑞 where 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].
Example 6.16 For all 𝑛 ⩾ 2, ℝ𝑛 with the standard topology is path-connected, and so is
ℝ𝑛 ⧵ {𝑝} for each 𝑝 ∈ ℝ𝑛 . For 𝑛 = 1, ℝ ⧵ {𝑝} is neither connected nor path-connected.
The next theorem states that path-connectedness implies connectedness. While the converse is not
true in general, it is true that open subsets of ℝ𝑛 that are connected (where ℝ𝑛 is given the standard
topology) are also path-connected. See [7, Proposition 12.25] for a proof of this fact.
Theorem 6.17 (Path-connectedness implies connectedness) Let 𝑋 be a path-connected
space. Then 𝑋 is connected.
Proof. Assume that 𝑋 is path-connected but that 𝑋 is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of 𝑋.
Let 𝑈 and 𝑉 be a separation of 𝑋. Let 𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋 be a path from 𝑥 ∈ 𝑈 to 𝑦 ∈ 𝑉 . Then 𝑓 −1 (𝑈 )
and 𝑓 −1 (𝑉 ) are disjoint non-empty open subsets of [𝑎, 𝑏] whose union is equal to [𝑎, 𝑏]. Thus they are
a separation of [𝑎, 𝑏]. This is a contradiction to the fact that [𝑎, 𝑏] is a connected space.

items.label: Exercise 6.1
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Show that if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is surjective
continuous map and 𝑋 is path-connected then 𝑌 is also path-connected.
We say that a subset 𝐴 of a topological space 𝑋 is path-connected if 𝐴 is path-connected in the
subspace topology. Hence, the previous exercise may be extended to saying that the continuous image
of a path-connected space is path-connected.

items.label: Exercise 6.2
items.statement:
Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 be subspaces of 𝑋.
(a) Show that if 𝐴 is connected then so is 𝐵.
(b) Show that [𝑎, 𝑏), (𝑎, 𝑏], [𝑎, 𝑏], (−∞, 𝑏] and [𝑎, ∞) are all connected spaces when considered
as subspaces of ℝ with the standard topology.

7. The fundamental group
7.1 Homotopy of paths
The topological properties we have discussed so far do not help us to distinguish between, e.g., the
2-sphere 𝑆 2 and the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 × 𝑆 1 . In order to prove that 𝑆 2 and 𝑇 2 are not topologically
equivalent, i.e., 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 , we need new properties and new techniques. A such property is that of
simple connectedness.
A path-connected space 𝑋 is, roughly speaking, simply connected if every closed curve in 𝑋 can be
shrunk to a point in 𝑋. The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected as any closed curve on 𝑆 2 can be shrunk to
a point in 𝑆 2 . On the other hand, the torus 𝑇 2 is not simply connected as there are closed curves which
cannot be shrunk to a point in 𝑇 2 . See Figure 7.1. To be simply connected is a topological property.
Hence, if 𝑋 and 𝑌 are homeomorphic topological spaces and one of them is simply connected then
so is the other. We can express that a path connected space is simply connected by saying that its
fundamental group is trivial. In particular, if 𝑋 ≅ 𝑌 then their fundamental groups are isomorphic.
We will show that the fundamental group of 𝑆 2 is not isomorphic to the fundamental group of 𝑇 2 .
Hence, 𝑆 2 ≇ 𝑇 2 . Computing fundamental groups provides us with more information than just that of
being simply connected.
Figure 7.1: The 2-sphere 𝑆 2 is simply connected while the torus 𝑇 2 = 𝑆 1 ×𝑆 1 is not simply connected.
In order to define the fundamental group of a topological space, we will need the concept of ho-
motopy (of paths).
Definition 7.1 (Homotopy) Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be two topological spaces, and let 𝑓0 , 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be
two continuous maps. Furthermore, let ℝ be the set of real numbers with the standard topology,
𝐼 = [0, 1] be a subspace of ℝ, and let 𝑋 × 𝐼 be given the product topology. We say that 𝑓0 is
homotopic to 𝑓1 , written 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 , if there is a continuous map
𝐻∶ 𝑋 ×𝐼 → 𝑌
such that 𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓0 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓1 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. The map 𝐻 is called a homotopy
between 𝑓0 and 𝑓1 . If 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 is a constant map, we say that 𝑓0 is nullhomotopic.

homotopy as it deforms for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 the point 𝑓0 (𝑥) to the point 𝑓1 (𝑥) along the straight-line

In particular, any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛 is homotopic to the constant map 𝑐 ∶ 𝑋 → ℝ𝑛
given by 𝑐(𝑥) = 0 for 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, any continuous map into ℝ𝑛 is nullhomotopic.

Theorem 7.4 The relation ≃ is an equivalence relation on the set of all continuous maps from
a topological space 𝑋 to a topological space 𝑌 .
Proof. We must show that ≃ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. For any continuous map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 →
𝑌 there is a homotopy 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 and all 𝑡 ∈ 𝐼. Then
𝐻(𝑥, 0) = 𝑓 (𝑥) and 𝐻(𝑥, 1) = 𝑓 (𝑥) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence, 𝑓 ≃ 𝑓 . Thus ≃ is reflexive.
Let 𝑓0 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 and 𝑓1 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be two continuous maps, and let 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy
between them. Then 𝐻 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by 𝐻(𝑥, 𝑡) = 𝐻(𝑥, 1 − 𝑡) is a homotopy between 𝑓1 and
𝑓0 . Hence, 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 implies 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓0 . Thus ≃ is symmetric.
To show that ≃ is transitive, we must show that if 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓1 and 𝑓1 ≃ 𝑓2 , then 𝑓0 ≃ 𝑓2 . Let
𝐻1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓1 , and let 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 be a homotopy from 𝑓1 to
𝑓2 . Then there is a map 𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐼 → 𝑌 given by

subsets of 𝑋 × 𝐼 whose union is equal to 𝑋 × 𝐼, and 𝐻1 (𝑥, 1) = 𝐻2 (𝑥, 0) for all 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Hence,
𝐻1 ∗ 𝐻2 is a homotopy from 𝑓0 to 𝑓2 . Thus ≃ is transitive.
Definition 7.5 (Homotopy classes) Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) be
the set of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 . The homotopy classes in 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) are the equivalence
classes under the relation ≃. We write [𝑓 ] for the homotopy class of 𝑓 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ), i.e.,
[𝑓 ] = {𝑔 ∈ 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 ) ∣ 𝑓 ≃ 𝑔},
and we write [𝑋, 𝑌 ] for the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 , i.e.,
[𝑋, 𝑌 ] = 𝐶(𝑋, 𝑌 )∕≃.
If we can connect two points 𝑥 and 𝑦 in a topological space 𝑋 by a path, i.e., a continuous map
𝑓 ∶ [𝑎, 𝑏] → 𝑋
such that 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑥 and 𝑓 (𝑏) = 𝑦, cf. Definition 6.14, we say that they lie in the same path component.
Specifically, the relation ∼𝑝 given by 𝑥 ∼𝑝 𝑦 if and only if there is a path from 𝑥 to 𝑦 in 𝑋, is an
equivalence relation on 𝑋. We say that an equivalence class in 𝑋 under ∼𝑝 is a path component. We
write 𝜋0 (𝑋) for the set of path components of 𝑋. Note that 𝜋0 (𝑋) consists of only one element if and
only if 𝑋 is path-connected.
Example 7.6 Let 𝑋 = {𝑥0 } and 𝑌 be topological spaces. Then the continuous maps

classes of continuous maps from 𝑋 to 𝑌 correspond to the path components of 𝑌 , and [𝑋, 𝑌 ]
corresponds to 𝜋0 (𝑌 ).
We are particularly interested in the case where we have homotopies between paths in a topological
space 𝑋. For simplicity, let all paths be continuous maps from 𝐼 = [0, 1] to 𝑋.

Definition 7.11 (Path homotopy classes) Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∈ 𝑋.

8. The fundamental group of the circle
8.1 Covering spaces
The notion of a covering space is a helpful tool for determining fundamental groups. Specifically, we
will compute the fundamental group of the circle by first establishing that the map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 , given
by
𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡)),
is a covering map.
Definition 8.1 (Covering spaces) Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. We say that a surjective
continuous map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map if for each point 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is a neighborhood
𝑈 such that 𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) is a disjoint union of open subsets 𝑉𝜆 of 𝐸 where 𝜆 ∈ Λ,
⨆
𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 ,
𝜆∈Λ
and 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ. We refer to 𝐸 as a covering space of
𝐵.
A covering map 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is sometimes also referred to as a covering projection. Note that 𝐵 is often
referred to as the base space of the covering map and that 𝐸 is often referred to as the total space of the covering
map. An open subset 𝑈 of 𝐵 satisfying the condition that
⨆
𝑝−1 (𝑈 ) = 𝑉𝜆 , where 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸,
𝜆∈Λ
such that 𝑝|𝑉𝜆 ∶ 𝑉𝜆 → 𝑈 is a homeomorphism for each 𝜆 ∈ Λ is said to be evenly covered by 𝑝. See Figure 8.1.
For each 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵, we often refer to the preimage 𝑝−1 (𝑏) of {𝑏} under 𝑝 as the fiber over 𝑏. Note also that the
fiber over 𝑏 is a discrete subspace of 𝐸; each 𝑉𝜆 is an open subset of 𝐸 and intersects the fiber over 𝑏 in a single
point, and so, this point is open in the fiber over 𝑏. Finally, we note that a covering map is a special case of a
fiber bundle with discrete fibers.
Example 8.2 Let 𝐵 and 𝐸 be topological spaces. If 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a homeomorphism, then 𝑝
is also a covering map.
Example 8.3 Let 𝑋 be a topological space, and let 𝐷 be a discrete space. Then the projection
map onto the first factor, pr 1 ∶ 𝑋 × 𝐷 → 𝑋, where 𝑋 × 𝐷 is given the product topology, is a
covering map. In particular, 𝑋 is evenly covered by pr 1 .

Definition 8.4 (Local homeomorphisms) Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces. A continuous
map 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is said to be a local homeomorphism if for each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 there is a neighborhood
𝑈 such that 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is open in 𝑌 and 𝑓 |𝑈 ∶ 𝑈 → 𝑓 (𝑈 ) is a homeomorphism where 𝑈 and 𝑓 (𝑈 )
are both given the subspace topology from 𝑋 and 𝑌 , respectively.
Example 8.5 Let 𝑋 and 𝑌 be topological spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 be a homeomorphism.
Then 𝑓 is also a local homeomorphism.
Local homeomorphisms preserve local topological properties such as local connectedness and local path-
connectedness. In other words, if 𝑓 ∶ 𝑋 → 𝑌 is a local homeomorphism and, say, 𝑋 is locally connected then
so is 𝑌 .
A topological space 𝑋 is locally (path) connected at 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋 if for each neighborhood 𝑈 of 𝑥 there is a (path)
connected neighborhood 𝑉 of 𝑥 such that 𝑉 ⊆ 𝑈 . We say that 𝑋 is locally (path) connected if 𝑋 is locally
(path) connected at each 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. Note that a (path) connected space need not be locally (path) connected.
Clearly, a covering map is also a local homeomorphism. Thus if 𝑝 ∶ 𝐸 → 𝐵 is a covering map, then the
total space and the base space are locally equivalent but they can differ globally.
The following theorem provides an important example of a covering space that we will use later
on to compute the fundamental group of the circle.
Theorem 8.6 Let ℝ be the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology, and
consider the circle 𝑆 1 as a subspace of ℝ2 where ℝ2 is given the standard topology. Then the
map 𝑝 ∶ ℝ → 𝑆 1 given by
𝑝(𝑡) = (cos(2𝜋𝑡), sin(2𝜋𝑡))
is a covering map.
82 8.1. Covering spaces
Proof. Clearly, 𝑝 is continuous and surjective. Let 𝑈 = 𝑆 1 ⧵ {(−1, 0)} and 𝑉 = 𝑆 1 ⧵ {(1, 0)}. Then

A. Set theory
The purpose of this appendix is not to give a complete and precise introduction to set theory but rather
to introduce some of the basic ideas, notation and terminologies from set theory that will be used
in our introduction to topology. We will follow naive set theory in the sense that we will describe
sets and operations on sets using natural language. The reader who is interested in a more thorough
introduction to set theory may wish to consult [4, Chapter 1] or [5, Appendix A]. For an even more
extensive treatment of set theory, see, e.g., [6].
A.1 Sets
In our naive approach, a set is any collection of mathematical objects, e.g., prime numbers. These
mathematical objects will be referred to as the elements of the set. We will typically denote sets using
uppercase letters, e.g., 𝑋, and elements of sets with lowercase letters, e.g., 𝑥. If 𝑋 is a set and 𝑥 an
element of 𝑋, we write 𝑥 ∈ 𝑋. If 𝑥 is not an element of 𝑋, we write 𝑥 ∉ 𝑋. In this notation, the
symbol “∈” denotes membership in a set. We will use the symbols ℕ, ℚ, ℝ and ℤ to denote the sets of
natural numbers, rational numbers, real numbers and integers, respectively. We will use the convention
that 0 is a natural number. We will use ℤ+ to denote the set of positive integers, i.e., integers greater
than or equal to 1.
Sets may be specified by listing its elements, e.g.,
𝑃 = {Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune}
for the set of planets in the Solar System. We may also specify sets by selecting the elements of some
previously given set that satisfy some well-defined condition, e.g.,
𝑆 = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ 𝑥 ⩾ 10.9}
read as “the set of 𝑥 ∈ ℝ such that 𝑥 ⩾ 10.9.” In this example, 129.3 ∈ 𝑆 while −1.5 ∉ 𝑆. Note that
some authors use colon instead of ∣ when describing sets in this way.
A set consisting of exactly one element is referred to as a singleton. If {𝑥} is the singleton consisting
of only the element 𝑥, then 𝑥 ∈ {𝑥} but 𝑥 is not equal to {𝑥} as the former is one object whereas the
latter is a set (consisting of one element) and these are not the same mathematical objects. We will
refer to the set of no elements as the empty set and denote it by ∅. If a set 𝑆 has one or more elements,
we will say that 𝑆 is non-empty.
Definition A.1 (Subsets) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets. We say that 𝐵 is a subset of 𝐴 if each element
of 𝐵 is also an element of 𝐴. We write 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴.
Note that some authors use the notation 𝐵 ⊂ 𝐴. We might also express that 𝐵 is a subset of 𝐴 by saying
that 𝐵 is contained in 𝐴. We say that 𝐴 is equal to 𝐵 if 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵 and 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴. If 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 but 𝐴 ≠ 𝐵 we will say that
𝐵 is a proper subset of 𝐴. We write 𝐵 ⊊ 𝐴.
95
96 A.1. Sets
Definition A.2 (Intersection and union of sets) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets. The intersection of 𝐴
and 𝐵, written 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵, is the set whose elements are both in 𝐴 and in 𝐵. That is,
𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 = {𝑥 ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵}.
The union of 𝐴 and 𝐵, written 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵, is the set whose elements are elements in 𝐴 or in 𝐵
where we do not exclude the possibility that an element in 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 is both an element in 𝐴 and
an element in 𝐵. That is,
𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 = {𝑥 ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 or 𝑥 ∈ 𝐵}
where “or” should be understood inclusively, i.e., 𝑥 is either an element in 𝐴 or in 𝐵 or in both.
Note that
𝐴∩𝐵 ⊆𝐴 𝐴⊆𝐴∪𝐵
𝐴∩𝐵 ⊆𝐵 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵.
We say that 𝐴 meets or intersects 𝐵 if 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 ≠ ∅. We say that 𝐴 and 𝐵 are disjoint if 𝐴 ∩ 𝐵 = ∅.
The following equations hold for all sets 𝐴, 𝐵 and 𝐶.
𝐴∩𝐵 =𝐵∩𝐴 (Commutative law for intersection)
𝐴∪𝐵 =𝐵∪𝐴 (Commutative law for union)
𝐴 ∩ (𝐵 ∩ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ∩ 𝐵) ∩ 𝐶 (Associative law for intersection)
𝐴 ∪ (𝐵 ∪ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ∪ 𝐵) ∪ 𝐶 (Associative law for union)
𝐴 ∩ (𝐵 ∪ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ∩ 𝐵) ∪ (𝐴 ∩ 𝐶) (Distributive law)
𝐴 ∪ (𝐵 ∩ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ∪ 𝐵) ∩ (𝐴 ∪ 𝐶) (Distributive law)
Each statement can be proved by showing that the left-hand side is a subset of the right-hand side and
vice versa. See, e.g., [6, p. 17] for a proof of the second distributive law using this principle.
Definition A.3 (Difference of sets) Let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝐵 be a subset of 𝐴. The difference
of 𝐴 and 𝐵, written 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵, is the set whose elements are in 𝐴 but not in 𝐵. That is,
𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵 = {𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 ∣ 𝑥 ∉ 𝐵}.
Note that some authors use the notation 𝐴 − 𝐵 for 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵. Also, note that
𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴, (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵) ∪ 𝐵 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵
and that 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵 and 𝐵 are disjoint, i.e., (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵) ∩ 𝐵 = ∅.
Definition A.4 (Complements) Let 𝐴 be a set, and let 𝐵 be a subset of 𝐴. The complement
of 𝐵 in 𝐴 is the difference of 𝐴 and 𝐵. We write 𝐵 𝑐 for the complement of 𝐵 in 𝐴, i.e.,
𝐵 𝑐 = 𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵.
Appendix A. Set theory 97
Assume that 𝐴, 𝐵 and 𝐶 are sets with 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴 and 𝐶 ⊆ 𝐴. Then the following equations, known as
De Morgan’s laws, hold.
𝐴 ⧵ (𝐵 ∩ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵) ∪ (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐶)
𝐴 ⧵ (𝐵 ∪ 𝐶) = (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐵) ∩ (𝐴 ⧵ 𝐶)
In our naive approach, a set is any collection of objects. Thus we might speak of a set of sets, that
is, a set whose elements are sets. We usually refer to such a set of sets as a collection of sets or as a
family of sets. We will usually speak of a family of sets when the elements in our set of sets are indexed
over some indexing set. Specifically, if 𝐼 is a set and {𝐴𝑖 ∣ 𝑖 ∈ 𝐼} is collection of sets, we will refer to
{𝐴𝑖 }𝑖∈𝐼 = {𝐴𝑖 ∣ 𝑖 ∈ 𝐼}
as an indexed family of sets and refer to 𝐼 as the indexing set for the family.
For any set 𝐴 we can speak of the power set of 𝐴. We write (𝐴). The power set of 𝐴 is the
collection of all subsets of 𝐴, i.e.,
(𝐴) = {𝐵 ∣ 𝐵 ⊆ 𝐴}.
Note that any collection of subsets of 𝐴 is a subset of (𝐴). We will usually denote collections using
script letters, e.g., .

98 A.2. Maps
Definition A.5 (Cartesian products) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets. The cartesian product of 𝐴 and
𝐵, written 𝐴 × 𝐵, is the set of ordered pairs of elements (𝑥, 𝑦) with 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵, i.e.,
𝐴 × 𝐵 = {(𝑥, 𝑦) ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 and 𝑦 ∈ 𝐵}.
Note that some authors denote elements in a cartesian product as 𝑥 × 𝑦 instead of (𝑥, 𝑦) primarily
to avoid any ambiguity that might arise when dealing with, e.g., open intervals of ℝ and elements in
ℝ2 = ℝ × ℝ.
A.2 Maps
In the following assume that 𝐴 and 𝐵 are two sets. A map (or function) 𝑓 from 𝐴 to 𝐵, written
𝑓∶ 𝐴→𝐵
is a rule that to each element 𝑎 in 𝐴 assigns a unique element 𝑓 (𝑎) in 𝐵. We refer to 𝐴 as the domain
(or source) of 𝑓 and to 𝐵 as the codomain (or target) of 𝑓 . We refer to the map
id ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐴
given by
id(𝑥) = 𝑥
for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 as the identity map on 𝐴. We will often write id𝐴 to emphasize the domain of the identity
map.
Definition A.6 (Image of maps) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵 be a map. The
image of 𝑓 is the subset
𝑓 (𝐴) = {𝑓 (𝑥) ∈ 𝐵 ∣ 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴}
of the codomain 𝐵, whose elements are all the values of 𝑓 .
Definition A.7 (Surjective, injective and bijective maps) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets, and let
𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵 be a map.
Surjective maps We say that 𝑓 is surjective if for every 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 there is an 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴 such that
𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑏. We will also refer to 𝑓 as a surjection.
Injective maps We say that 𝑓 is injective if 𝑓 (𝑎1 ) = 𝑓 (𝑎2 ) if and only if 𝑎1 = 𝑎2 where
𝑎1 , 𝑎2 ∈ 𝐴. We will also refer to 𝑓 as an injection.
Bijective maps We say that 𝑓 is bijective if it is both injective and surjective. We will
also refer to 𝑓 as a bijection (or as a one-to-one correspondence).
Note that 𝑓 is surjective if and only if 𝑓 (𝐴) = 𝐵. If 𝑓 is bijective there is for each 𝑏 ∈ 𝐵 exactly one 𝑎 ∈ 𝐴
with 𝑓 (𝑎) = 𝑏, and we can define a map 𝑓 −1 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝐴 given by 𝑓 −1 (𝑏) = 𝑎. We say that 𝑓 −1 is the inverse map
of 𝑓 . Note that the inverse map is not defined when 𝑓 is not bijective.
Appendix A. Set theory 99
Definition A.8 (Restriction of maps) Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵 be a map. The
restriction of 𝑓 to 𝑅 where 𝑅 ⊆ 𝐴 is the map
𝑓 |𝑅 ∶ 𝑅 → 𝐵
given by
𝑓 |𝑅 (𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥)
for 𝑥 ∈ 𝑅. Let 𝐶 ⊆ 𝐵 and assume that 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐶. The restriction of 𝑓 to 𝐶 is the map
𝑔∶ 𝐴 → 𝐶
given by
𝑔(𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥)
for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴.
Note that the restriction of 𝑓 to 𝐶 is not defined unless 𝑓 (𝐴) ⊆ 𝐶. If 𝑅 ⊆ 𝐴 and 𝑓 |𝑅 is injective, then the
restriction of 𝑓 |𝑅 to 𝐶 = 𝑓 |𝑅 (𝑅) is bijective.
Definition A.9 (Composition of maps) Let 𝐴, 𝐵 and 𝐶 be sets, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵 and
𝑔 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝐶 be maps. The composition of 𝑓 and 𝑔, written 𝑔◦𝑓 , is the map
𝑔◦𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐶
given by
(𝑔◦𝑓 )(𝑥) = 𝑔(𝑓 (𝑥))
for 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴.
Note that composition is associative, i.e., if 𝐷 is a set and ℎ ∶ 𝐶 → 𝐷 is a map, we have
ℎ◦(𝑔◦𝑓 ) = (ℎ◦𝑔)◦𝑓 .
Also, 𝑓 ◦ id𝐴 = 𝑓 = id𝐵 ◦𝑓 .
The following two theorems describe properties of bijective maps. Proofs are immediate and left
as an exercise to the reader.
Theorem A.10 Let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be sets, and let 𝑓 ∶ 𝐴 → 𝐵 be a map. Then 𝑓 is bijective if and
only if there is a map 𝑔 ∶ 𝐵 → 𝐴 such that
𝑔◦𝑓 = id𝐴 and 𝑓 ◦𝑔 = id𝐵 .
The map 𝑔 is then the inverse of 𝑓 , i.e., 𝑔 = 𝑓 −1 .

B. Elementary algebra
B.1 Groups
Groups are sets with a binary operation satisfying certain axioms, and frequently appear in relation to
symmetry.
Definition B.1 (Binary operations) Let 𝑆 be a set. A binary operation on 𝑆 is a map
∗ ∶ 𝑆 × 𝑆 → 𝑆. We write 𝑎 ∗ 𝑏 instead of ∗ (𝑎, 𝑏) for 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝑆.
The definition says that the set 𝑆 is closed under the binary operation ∗ in the sense that if 𝑎 and 𝑏
are elements in 𝑆, then so is 𝑎 ∗ 𝑏.
Example B.2 Let ℤ be the set of integers. Addition (+) and multiplication (⋅) are two binary
operations on ℤ.
Definition B.3 (Groups) A group is a non-empty set 𝐺 together with a binary operation ∗
such that the following properties hold.
G1 For all 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐 ∈ 𝐺, (𝑎 ∗ 𝑏) ∗ 𝑐 = 𝑎 ∗ (𝑏 ∗ 𝑐).
G2 There is an element 𝑒 ∈ 𝐺 such that 𝑎 ∗ 𝑒 = 𝑎 = 𝑒 ∗ 𝑎 for all 𝑎 ∈ 𝐺.
G3 For each 𝑎 ∈ 𝐺 there is an element 𝑎′ ∈ 𝐺 such that 𝑎 ∗ 𝑎′ = 𝑒 = 𝑎′ ∗ 𝑎.
We say that a group is abelian if ∗ is also commutative: 𝑎 ∗ 𝑏 = 𝑏 ∗ 𝑎 for all 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ 𝐺.
A group is strictly speaking an ordered pair (𝐺, ∗). We often omit specific mention of ∗ if no confusion
will arise. The first axiom, G1, says that ∗ is an associative operation. The second axiom, G2, says that there
is a neutral or identity element for ∗. The third axiom, G3, says that for each element 𝑎 ∈ 𝐺 there is an inverse
element 𝑎′ ∈ 𝐺 such that 𝑎′ is both a left and right inverse element, i.e., 𝑎′ ∗ 𝑎 = 𝑒 and 𝑎 ∗ 𝑎′ = 𝑒, respectively.
The order of a group 𝐺 is the number of elements in 𝐺, written |𝐺|.
We say that a group is written multiplicatively if we write 𝑎𝑏 instead of 𝑎 ∗ 𝑏, 1 (sometimes) instead of 𝑒 and
𝑎−1 instead of 𝑎′ , and we say that a group is written additively if we write 𝑎 + 𝑏 instead of 𝑎 ∗ 𝑏, 0 (sometimes)
instead of 𝑒 and −𝑎 instead of 𝑎′ .
Example B.4 The set of real numbers, ℝ, is an abelian group under addition: G1, G2 and G3
are all satisfied with 𝑒 = 0 and the inverse element of 𝑎 ∈ ℝ being −𝑎. Furthermore, it is an
abelian group as 𝑎 + 𝑏 = 𝑏 + 𝑎 for all 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ ℝ.
Note that ℝ is not a group under multiplication: while G1 and G2 are satisfied (with 𝑒 = 1),
G3 fails. There is no real number we can multiply with 0 to get 1.

Definition B.7 (Subgroups) Let 𝐺 be a group with binary operation ∗. We say that a subset
𝐻 of 𝐺 is a subgroup, written 𝐻 ⩽ 𝐺, if ∗ restricted to 𝐻 × 𝐻 is a binary operation on 𝐻
such that 𝐻 with ∗ |𝐻×𝐻 is a group. We say that a subgroup 𝐻 of 𝐺 is a proper subgroup of
𝐺, written 𝐻 < 𝐺, if 𝐻 is a proper subset of 𝐺.
The set consisting of just the identity element is always a subgroup of a group. It is often called
the trivial subgroup.
Example B.8 Let 𝐺 = ℤ with addition as its binary operation. For 𝑚 ∈ ℤ+ ⧵ {1}, let
𝐻 = 𝑚ℤ = {𝑚𝑛 ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ} = {… , −2𝑚, −𝑚, 0, 𝑚, 2𝑚, …}.
Then 𝐻 is a proper subgroup of 𝐺.
Theorem B.9 Let 𝐺 be a group that is written multiplicatively, and let 𝑥 ∈ 𝐺. If 𝐻 is a
subgroup of 𝐺 containing 𝑥, then
⟨𝑥⟩ = {𝑥𝑛 ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}
is a subgroup of 𝐻.
We say that ⟨𝑥⟩ is the cyclic subgroup of 𝐺 generated by 𝑥. Note that ⟨𝑥⟩ is the smallest subgroup of 𝐺
containing 𝑥. If there is an 𝑥′ ∈ 𝐺 such that 𝐺 = ⟨𝑥′ ⟩, we say that 𝐺 is cyclic, and that 𝑥′ is a generator for 𝐺.
Note that cyclic groups may have more than one generators and that all cyclic groups are abelian. If the group
is written additively, we interpret ⟨𝑥⟩ as the set {𝑛𝑥 ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ}.
Proof. Since 𝐻 is a subgroup of 𝐺, it follows that 𝑥−1 ∈ 𝐻. Thus 𝑥𝑥−1 = 𝑥−1 𝑥 = 𝑥0 = 1 ∈ 𝐻.
Furthermore, 𝑥𝑛 ∈ 𝐻 for all 𝑛 ∈ ℤ where 𝑥−𝑚 = (𝑥−1 )𝑚 . Thus ⟨𝑥⟩ is a subgroup of 𝐻.

Example B.10 The set of integers, ℤ, is an infinite cyclic group with addition as its binary
operation generated by both −1 and 1, i.e., ⟨−1⟩ = ⟨1⟩ = ℤ.
Definition B.11 (Left and right cosets) Let 𝐺 be a group, and let 𝐻 be a subgroup of 𝐺.
For 𝑔 ∈ 𝐺, we say that the set
𝑔 ∗ 𝐻 = {𝑔 ∗ ℎ ∣ ℎ ∈ 𝐻}
is the left coset of 𝐻 containing 𝑔, and that the set
𝐻 ∗ 𝑔 = {ℎ ∗ 𝑔 ∣ ℎ ∈ 𝐻}
is the right coset of 𝐻 containing 𝑔.
Clearly, if 𝐺 is abelian, the left and right cosets are equal. However, the left and right cosets may
coincide even if 𝐺 is not abelian.
Definition B.12 (Normal subgroups) Let 𝐺 be a group. We say that a subgroup 𝐻 of 𝐺 is
normal if the left and right cosets coincide, i.e., 𝑔 ∗ 𝐻 = 𝐻 ∗ 𝑔 for all 𝑔 ∈ 𝐺.
An equivalent definition of a normal subgroup is to say that 𝐻 is a normal subgroup of 𝐺 if 𝑔 ∗
ℎ ∗ 𝑔 −1 ∈ 𝐻 for each 𝑔 ∈ 𝐺 and each ℎ ∈ 𝐻.

B.2 Homomorphisms
Homomorphisms are structure preserving maps from one group to another.
Definition B.15 (Homomorphisms) Let 𝐺1 and 𝐺2 be groups with binary operations ∗1 and
∗2 , respectively. Then a map 𝜑 ∶ 𝐺1 → 𝐺2 is a homomorphism if

108 B.2. Homomorphisms
Definition B.19 (Isomorphisms) Let 𝐺1 and 𝐺2 be groups. We say that a bijective homo-
morphism 𝜑 ∶ 𝐺1 → 𝐺2 is an isomorphism.
Thus a homomorphism which is both a monomorphism and an epimorphism is an isomorphism.

concept_map.description: Concept-level map of spaces, definitions, examples, and motivating relations.
concept_map.example_traversal: topological_space -> defined_by -> topology; compactness -> has_example -> cantor_set; quotient_space_examples -> motivates -> quotient_topology

definition_dependency_graph.description: Definitions needed to state concepts, structures, and theorems.
definition_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set

theorem_dependency_graph.description: Logical dependencies among results and assumptions, separated from definitional needs.
theorem_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> uses -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> uses -> zorns_lemma; tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice

proof_graph.description: Proofs, proof steps, proof methods, and results used by particular proofs.

inheritance_tree.description: Tree-like or DAG-like inheritance view of mathematical structures; multiple inheritance is retained.
inheritance_tree.example_traversal: incoming subtype_of from topological_space, then from compact_space and hausdorff_space to compact_hausdorff_space

construction_graph.description: How spaces and topologies are constructed from prior data.
construction_graph.example_traversal: product_topology -> constructed_from -> subbasis; product_topology -> induces -> product_space

prerequisite_map.description: Pedagogical order; useful for learning but not always logical necessity.
prerequisite_map.example_traversal: set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem

application_map.description: Where theorems, methods, and constructions are applied or illustrated.
application_map.example_traversal: brouwer_fixed_point_theorem -> applied_to -> fixed_point_applications; jordan_curve_theorem -> has_example -> circle

traversal_primitives.outgoing: Follow edges whose source is the current node.
traversal_primitives.incoming: Follow edges whose target is the current node.
traversal_primitives.both: Follow incoming and outgoing edges while preserving direction in returned paths.
traversal_primitives.multigraph_rule: Do not collapse parallel edges; edge type and dependency_class determine meaning.

dependency_semantics.definitions_needed_to_state: requires edges with dependency_class definitional_dependency or notation_dependency
dependency_semantics.proof_tools_used: uses edges from theorem, proof, or proof_step to proof_method, lemma, theorem, proposition, or axiom

example_queries.example: From tychonoff_theorem return product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set, product_notation, open_cover_notation, alexander_subbase_theorem, zorns_lemma, and axiom_of_choice grouped by dependency_class.
From urysohn_metrization_theorem follow requires to regularity, hausdorff_property, second_countability, and metric_topology.
From heine_borel_theorem keep targets of kind proof_method: open_cover_argument, finite_subcover_argument, compactness_argument.
From tychonoff_theorem follow assumes to axiom_of_choice; from urysohn_metrization_theorem follow assumes to regularity_assumption, hausdorff_assumption, and second_countability_assumption.
From compactness follow has_example to cantor_set and circle; also inspect incoming has_property from examples for additional satisfying instances.
From hausdorff_assumption use incoming shows_necessity_of to get non_hausdorff_spaces_as_counterexamples, then inspect compact_subsets_of_hausdorff_are_closed -> has_counterexample.
From topological_space follow incoming subtype_of; compact_hausdorff_space appears under both compact_space and hausdorff_space.

One path is set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem via prerequisite_for; enrich with tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_topology and tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice.
